name: Suriname
excerpt: The Spaniards first explored Suriname in the 16th century, and the English then settled it in the mid-17th century. Suriname became a Dutch colony in 1667. With the abolition of African slavery in 1863, workers were brought in from India and Java.

quick_facts.religions: Protestant 23.6% (includes Evangelical 11.2%, Moravian 11.2%, Reformed 0.7%, Lutheran 0.5%), Hindu 22.3%, Roman Catholic 21.6%, Muslim 13.8%, other Christian 3.2%, Winti 1.8%, Jehovah's Witness 1.2%, other 1.7%, none 7.5%, unspecified 3.2% (2012 est.)
quick_facts.ethnic_groups: Hindustani (also known locally as "East Indians"; their ancestors emigrated from northern India in the latter part of the 19th century) 27.4%, Maroon (their African ancestors were brought to the country in the 17th and 18th centuries as slaves and escaped to the interior) 21.7%, Creole (mixed White and Black) 15.7%, Javanese 13.7%, mixed 13.4%, other 7.6%, unspecified 0.6% (2012 est.)
quick_facts.terrain: mostly rolling hills; narrow coastal plain with swamps
quick_facts.natural_resources: timber, hydropower, fish, kaolin, shrimp, bauxite, gold, and small amounts of nickel, copper, platinum, iron ore
quick_facts.major_exports: gold, fish, refined petroleum, wood, tobacco (2023)

summaries.geography_summary: smallest independent country on the South American continent; mostly tropical rainforest; great diversity of flora and fauna; relatively small population, mostly along the coast
summaries.history_summary: The Spaniards first explored Suriname in the 16th century, and the English then settled it in the mid-17th century. Suriname became a Dutch colony in 1667. With the abolition of African slavery in 1863, workers were brought in from India and Java. The Netherlands granted the colony independence in 1975. Five years later, the civilian government was replaced by a military regime that soon declared Suriname a socialist republic. It continued to exert control through a succession of nominally civilian administrations until 1987, when international pressure finally forced a democratic election. In 1990, the military overthrew the civilian leadership, but a democratically elected government -- a four-party coalition -- returned to power in 1991. The coalition expanded to eight parties in 2005 and ruled until 2010, when voters returned former military leader Desire BOUTERSE and his opposition coalition to power. President BOUTERSE ran unopposed in 2015 and was reelected. Opposition parties campaigned hard against BOUTERSE in the run-up to the 2020 elections, and a multi-party coalition led by Chandrikapersad SANTOKHI’s VHP and Ronnie Brunswijk’s ABOP was installed.
summaries.government_summary: presidential republic. Independence: 25 November 1975 (from the Netherlands)
summaries.economy_summary: upper middle-income South American economy; new floating currency regime; key aluminum goods, gold, and hydrocarbon exporter; new IMF plan for economic recovery and fiscal sustainability; controversial hardwood industry

suggested_reading: CIA World Factbook data (archived via factbook.json) | https://github.com/factbook/factbook.json

prayer_and_mission.prayer_intro: Suriname is remarkably religiously diverse, with substantial Protestant, Hindu, Catholic, and Muslim communities. Pray for the clear gospel to reach across this multiethnic, multireligious nation.
prayer_points: Pray for Suriname's Protestant and Evangelical churches to be equipped with sound, Scripture-centered doctrine.
Pray for gospel witness to reach Suriname's Hindu community.
Pray for gospel witness among Suriname's Muslim community.
Pray for pastors to be trained in expository preaching.
prayer_and_mission.mission_emphasis: Suriname's remarkable religious diversity, with substantial Protestant, Hindu, Catholic, and Muslim communities side by side, makes it a genuinely complex field. Ongoing needs include training national pastors and reaching Suriname's Hindu and Muslim communities with the clear gospel.
